semanticCore: Midday nap, speaking, ruler, rest place, shade, or say
quranicRelevance: Midday resting place in Paradise (Maqeel - 'ahsana maqeelan'), and naptime destruction ('qa'iloon').

keyDerivations.meaning: Midday resting place, nap location, shaded room, or cool shelter
Those taking a midday nap, resting at noon, or sleeping

figurativeOrRareUsages: أَصْحَابُ الْجَنَّةِ يَوْمئِذٍ خَيْرٌ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَأَحْسَنُ مَقِيلًا (24:24): The companions of Paradise on that Day will be in a better settlement and a more beautiful midday resting place (Maqeel).
discardedNoise: Speaking and saying (Qawl) - conceptually related but distinct root forms (q-w-l vs q-y-l).
Tribal kings (Qayl) of Himyar in Yemen history.
summaryEn: The root represents midday rest (Qaylulah), promising the saved a beautiful, shaded resting place (Maqeel) in Paradise, while warning that destruction hit past nations during their naptime.
summaryAr: يمثل هذا الجذر الاستراحة والمنام بنصف النهار (قيلولة)، واصفاً نعيم استراحة أهل الجنة ساعة القيلولة الخفيفة المبردة (أحسن مقيلاً)، وهلاك الأمم نهاراً.
rawText: أَقَالَ ٱللّٰهُ عَثْرَتَكَ 4 , and عِثاَرَكَ, [May God cancel thy slip, lapse, fault, wrong action, or mistake: (A, art. عثر:) may God raise thee from thy fall. (Msb, art. قيل.) أَقَالَهُ عَثْرَتَهُ He forgave him his slip, lapse, or fault. (MA.) اِسْتَقاَلَ البَيْعَ 10 He desired, or demanded, the rescinding of the sale, or purchase. (MA.) And استقال العَنْوَةُ He desired, or demanded, his passing over, or forgiving, the slip, lapse, or fault. (MA.) See also Har, p. 7. See also a verse cited voce عَنْوَةٌ. تَقَيَّلَ أَبَاهُ 5 : see تَقَيَّضَ and تَأَسَّلَ. قَائِلَةٌ : see غَاَئِرَهٌ. قَالَ 1 He slept during midday: (Mgh:) or he stayed during midday. (TA, art. هجر.) === قَيَّلَ: see another meaning, voce بَيَّتَ. قَايَلَهُ البَيْعَ 3 [He dissolved, rescinded, or annulled, with him the sale]. (A, art. رد.) مَقِيلٌ A resting-place; syn. مُسْتَقَرٌّ: hence, مَقِيلُ الحُبِّ [the resting-place of love] and مَقِيلُ الغَيْظِ [the resting-place of wrath], applied by El-Mutanebbee to the heart. (W, i. 112.) See an ex. (mistranslated) in De Sacy's Ar. Gr., sec. ed., ii. 165: the same, with a var., in Ibn-Akeel p. 210.
